title: Sorting Using Partial Information
speaker: Robert Tarjan
abstract:
We consider the problem of sorting a set of items having an unknown total order by doing binary comparisons of the items, given the outcomes of some pre-existing comparisons. We present a simple new algorithm with a running time of O(m + n + log T), where n, m, and T are the number of items, the number of pre-existing comparisons, and the number of total orders consistent with the outcomes of the pre-existing comparisons, respectively. The algorithm does O(log T) comparisons. Both our time and comparison bounds are best possible up to constant factors, thus resolving a problem that has been open since 1978. Our algorithm combines two classic algorithms: topological sort and heapsort with the right kind of heap.
This is joint work with Bernhard Haeupler, Richard Hladík, John lacono, Vaclay Rozhoi, and Jakub Tětek.

proof_stages.label: Partial-information sorting as DAG linear extensions
proof_stages.summary: After recalling the comparison-sorting lower bound, the talk models known comparisons as a DAG/poset whose feasible sorted orders are topological orders, giving the lower bound log T and setting the goal of matching it with near-linear overhead.

proof_stages.label: Topological heapsort reduction
proof_stages.summary: Kahn's source-removal algorithm for topological ordering is converted into a sorting algorithm by keeping current sources in a priority queue and repeatedly deleting the true minimum source.

proof_stages.label: Working-set heaps as the adaptive ingredient
proof_stages.summary: The inadequacy of ordinary heap bounds motivates a working-set, then strict working-set, cost measure for delete-min operations, and known self-adjusting heap/search-tree implementations provide these amortized guarantees.

proof_stages.label: Interval-graph lower bound for heap work
proof_stages.summary: For a fixed run, vertices are indexed by heap-insertion time and mapped to intervals whose lengths are their working-set sizes; an interval-order lemma lower-bounds the relevant number of topological orders by the product of these lengths, bounding total heap work by the information-theoretic optimum up to an additive n.

proof_stages.label: Bottleneck-layer refinement removing additive n
proof_stages.summary: When log T is small the DAG must contain a long path and many singleton layers, so the algorithm identifies comparison-free bottleneck vertices, sorts only the remaining vertices, and merges the two streams to remove the additive n in the comparison bound.

proof_stages.label: Top-k extension via strict working sets
proof_stages.summary: By generating bottlenecks and merge output incrementally and using heaps whose strict working-set cost ignores items never deleted, the method reports the first k elements within a constant factor of the appropriate top-k lower bound.

proof_stages.label: Scope, limitations, and related adaptive optimality
proof_stages.summary: The speaker summarizes the sorting and top-k theorems, notes that the guarantee is amortized rather than a solution to the one-third/two-third comparison-split conjecture, and discusses input-size dependence, applications, randomization, and related instance-optimal shortest-path questions.

examples.name: Lettered DAG with redundant and implied arcs
examples.note: A concrete DAG whose hidden sorted order is alphabetical is used to display redundant transitive arcs, possible topological orders, Kahn-source choices, and heap deletions, making the poset model concrete without driving the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks redundant and implied arcs in the lettered DAG and later runs source/topological-sort and heap steps on it

examples.name: Long path plus incomparable singletons
examples.note: A long chain together with k isolated incomparable vertices shows why an ordinary heap can waste n log k comparisons while only newly inserted path vertices matter, visibly motivating the working-set bound and later the bottleneck refinement.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the long path plus singletons example, deriving heap contents and comparison costs such as n log k versus n+k log k

passes.reason: A chain plus isolated incomparable vertices has trivial components and serves as a bad case for the standard heap approach.

examples.name: Dijkstra shortest-path ordering problem
examples.note: The speaker says the work originated from trying to prove an instance-optimal version of Dijkstra's algorithm for reporting vertices by distance, which forced adaptive heaps with efficient decrease-key operations and suggested the sorting application.
examples.engagement_reason: the Dijkstra shortest-path ordering problem is described as a related application, but no concrete graph instance or computation is worked through
